title: How to Work with Nausea During the Come-Up Phase
body:
How to Work with Nausea During the Come-Up Phase

1. That Queasy Feeling: A Personal Story

I still remember the first time nausea hit me during a mushroom ceremony. It was early in my psychedelic journey, and I was eager, maybe a little too eager, to jump into the experience. Right after I took the dose, a slow churn began in my stomach. It crept up quietly, like a warning whisper, but soon it became a roar. I felt sweaty, uneasy, and the part of my brain that wanted control kept screaming, “Get away from this feeling!” I wasn’t ready for this. Was I doing something wrong? Was this a sign to stop?

Many people new to psychedelics feel blindsided by nausea. They expect the “mystical” parts, the visuals, emotional release, but the gut reaction feels like a betrayal. It’s hard to lean into a feeling that seems so physical, so raw. But much like the early stages of any personal growth, discomfort marks the start of transformation. When I talk about nausea now, I’m speaking from the knowing that it’s not just a side effect; it’s a signal, a message from our biology that something intense is unfolding.

It reminds me of a story from Stanislav Grof’s early work. The pioneers of psychedelic therapy weren’t just focused on the mental adventures but the body’s language too. Grof called this the “perinatal matrix,” where the body and mind are deeply intertwined. Sometimes the body releases old tension through sensations like nausea. So if nausea arrives, it’s not a bug in the system; it’s part of the unfolding process. Instead of trying to make it vanish, I learned to listen, breathe, and sometimes even invite the queasiness like a guest. This shift in relationship made all the difference in how I moved through sessions, and I want to share that with you here.

2. Why Nausea Happens: The Science Behind It

Understanding nausea during the mushroom come-up starts with looking at what’s happening in the body and brain. When psilocybin enters the system, it quickly converts into psilocin, which hooks into serotonin receptors, especially the 5-HT2A receptor, a key player in how psychedelics change consciousness. But serotonin is also heavily involved in the gut’s nervous system - the enteric nervous system, often called our “second brain.”

The gut is packed with serotonin receptors, and they regulate motility, secretion, and overall digestive health. When psilocin floods these receptors, it’s no surprise that some digestive disruption follows. The body’s response can include nausea, a kind of protective reflex trying to expel what it senses as a toxin. Mushrooms, after all, are technically fungi, and the body’s default might be to reject them.

One way to think of nausea is as a cross-talk between brain and gut. The vagus nerve, a major conduit between these two organs, carries signals back and forth constantly. When psychedelics activate certain brain areas - like the insula, which monitors bodily states - it can heighten awareness of gut sensations, making nausea feel more intense. This is not just a simple stomach upset but a complex mind-body dialogue happening in real time.

Studies in neurogastroenterology, like those reviewed by Gershon in his work on the “second brain,” show how emotional states and gut feelings are deeply intertwined. This links with what psychedelic researchers like Robin Carhart-Harris have found, where psychedelics temporarily loosen the brain’s rigid networks, allowing for new connections to form. This includes heightened sensitivity to bodily states, including any queasiness.

Another factor is the mushroom’s physical makeup. Psilocybin mushrooms contain chitin, a tough fiber that’s hard to digest. Many people find that eating whole mushrooms or poorly prepared ones increases nausea. This is part of why some practitioners prefer extracts or teas - they reduce the physical burden on the digestive system.

Finally, nausea may also arise from the body’s detox pathways kicking in. Psychedelic therapies often release stored tension and emotional blocks, as Bessel van der Kolk describes in The Body Keeps the Score. The body can respond with physical symptoms, nausea among them, as it adjusts to shifts in emotional and physiological states. This gives nausea a meaningful role beyond the mere physical reaction.

3. What Science and Research Say About Nausea and Psychedelics

While the psychedelic renaissance has mainly focused on therapeutic outcomes like depression or PTSD, researchers do touch on the physical side effects, nausea included. A 2017 study led by Roland Griffiths at Johns Hopkins - famous for proving psilocybin’s safety and efficacy - reported that mild to moderate nausea was a common transient effect among participants. It didn’t overshadow the lasting benefits, but it was one of the discomforts folks expected during the come-up.

Similarly, Carhart-Harris’ team at Imperial College London noted in their research that nausea was frequent but manageable when participants had proper preparation and supportive settings. One key takeaway was that the psychological context - the so-called “set and setting” - modulates how physical symptoms are experienced. The same nausea can feel terrifying or just mildly uncomfortable depending on mindset and external support.

In a paper published in the Journal of Psychopharmacology, researchers compared different routes of psilocybin administration. Oral doses that included raw mushroom material often caused more nausea than purified psilocybin capsules or brewed teas. This matched anecdotal reports from my own circle of mushroom workers and therapists, confirming that preparation methods matter.

Stanislav Grof’s early work, although more clinical and less focused on nausea specifically, highlighted how physical discomfort during psychedelic sessions points to deeper psychophysical processes. His concept of “holotropic breathwork” mirrors some aspects of how to work with discomfort in psychedelic therapy. Through breathing and attention, physical symptoms like nausea can be transmuted rather than avoided.

Recently, some clinicians have advocated for using anti-nausea remedies before or during sessions. Ginger, for example, stands out as a natural option with a strong evidence base in conventional medicine for combating nausea. A 2015 review in the Journal of Pain and Symptom Management pointed out ginger’s efficacy and safety. However, there’s debate about whether suppressing nausea pharmacologically might blunt the psychedelic experience’s authenticity.

All this scientific insight shows that nausea is a recognized and studied part of the psychedelic process, not just anecdotal noise. It’s woven into the neurochemistry, biology, and therapeutic journey. Embracing this knowledge helps us approach nausea not with fear but with curiosity and practical tools.

4. How to Apply These Insights and Work with Nausea

Here’s where the rubber meets the road. When nausea shows up during your mushroom come-up, what can you do? Based on years of experience and evidence, I’ve found a few practical strategies that can make a huge difference.

First, preparation matters. Eating light meals an hour or two before dosing reduces the risk of nausea. Heavy or greasy foods tend to sit in your stomach longer, increasing discomfort. Many seasoned psychonauts prefer to fast or have a small, easy-to-digest snack. Brewing mushrooms into a tea, as Paul Stamets recommends, can also help. The tea extracts psilocybin without much chitin, making it gentler on the gut.

Once nausea begins, try to stay calm and breathe deeply. The breath is a powerful tool that Grof emphasized in his work. Slow, steady breathing helps downregulate the autonomic nervous system, reducing the fight-or-flight response that can worsen nausea. You can use simple techniques like inhaling for a count of four, holding for two, and exhaling for six. This rhythmic breathing shifts attention away from the queasiness and invites relaxation.

Hydration is key, but sip water slowly to avoid upsetting your stomach further. Some people find that sucking on a small piece of crystallized ginger or drinking ginger tea helps ease nausea. Peppermint is another natural aid that calms the stomach lining.

Another powerful approach is to shift your mindset about nausea. Instead of seeing it as a punishment or failure, view it as a signal your body is working. This mindset shift alone can reduce anxiety around the feeling. Journaling your sensations can deepen this process. I often recommend keeping a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound nearby to jot down physical sensations and emotional shifts during the come-up. Writing helps externalize discomfort and track progress over time.

Don’t hesitate to ask for support if you’re in a group or therapeutic setting. Trusted guides or therapists can provide grounding presence, remind you to breathe, or offer reassurance. Michael Pollan highlights in How to Change Your Mind how the social container can change the quality of the experience dramatically.

Lastly, gentle movement can help. Some people feel better walking slowly around or rocking side to side. If you feel up to it, mild stretching or yoga poses aimed at opening the abdomen can release tension. But listen closely to your body; if movement worsens nausea, rest instead.

5. Facing Common Challenges and Working Through Them

Nausea during the come-up isn’t always predictable or uniform. Some days it’s barely noticeable; other times it feels overwhelming. One challenge is the fear nausea can spark. When your stomach rebels, your mind often follows with thoughts of vomiting or panic. This fear feeds the nausea, creating a self-sustaining loop. Breaking that loop requires patience and self-compassion.

Sometimes people try to suppress nausea with over-the-counter remedies or prescription meds without considering effects on the psychedelic process. Antiemetics like ondansetron can be helpful but might dull the experience or interfere with serotonin receptors. I encourage cautious use and consultation with knowledgeable guides if considering medication.

Another challenge is the physical persistence of nausea. Sometimes it lasts well into the peak phase, making it hard to fully relax. In those cases, focusing on the breath and gentle distractions helps. I often suggest grounding techniques like feeling the texture of a blanket or focusing on ambient sounds to redirect attention.

For some, nausea triggers past trauma stored in the gut, a concept Bessel van der Kolk explores deeply in his work. This means nausea can pull up emotional material that feels overwhelming. Here, the key is to acknowledge rather than suppress the feelings. Sitting with them, breathing through them, and perhaps journaling can facilitate release. Working with a therapist trained in trauma or psychedelic integration can provide invaluable support.

Finally, cultural or personal expectations about psychedelics can make nausea feel like failure. Many newcomers expect a blissful, euphoric trip and don’t anticipate physical discomfort. When nausea arrives, they may feel ashamed or disappointed. Normalizing nausea as part of the journey helps counter this stigma. Sharing experiences in community settings or support groups can remind you that you’re not alone.

6. Integrating Nausea Lessons into Daily Life

Nausea during the come-up doesn’t exist in a vacuum. It’s often a mirror for how we relate to discomfort in life. The lessons learned sitting with nausea can ripple out into daily living in surprising ways.

For starters, working through nausea can teach radical acceptance. Rather than fighting discomfort or wishing it away, you practice allowing it. This softening toward difficulty can carry through to stressful moments off the medicine - work tension, relationship challenges, or emotional upheavals. The patience developed with a queasy stomach is a muscle you build for life.

Additionally, paying attention to bodily signals during mushroom experiences often opens a doorway to better body awareness overall. You might notice subtle cues in digestion, hunger, or tension you previously ignored. This increased interoception helps you respond earlier and more kindly to your body’s needs.

Another integration area is emotional resilience. Sometimes nausea brings up old stored feelings, which surface in the session and then release. Carrying this forward, you might find yourself less reactive to emotional “nausea” in everyday life. The physical experience becomes a metaphor for emotional waves that pass.

For anyone committed to long-term well-being, the lessons around nausea support a daily practice of self-care and reflection. Journaling regularly - using tools like the Journaling Notebook Leather Bound - can help maintain connection to your body’s messages. Recording small discomforts and how you respond sharpens your self-awareness.

Finally, this process can deepen your trust in natural processes. Just as the body sometimes needs to cleanse or recalibrate, challenging sensations like nausea remind us that healing and growth aren’t always comfortable. They ask us to slow down, listen, and stay present. These are timeless lessons, invaluable whether you’re sitting on a cushion or navigating life’s twists and turns.

7. A New Thought to Sit With: The Gift of Queasiness

Here’s a thought I don’t offer lightly: nausea during the come-up might be one of psychedelic medicine’s secret gifts. On the surface, it’s an unpleasant feeling, a warning light flashing on your internal dashboard. But beneath that, it’s a deep invitation. It asks you to tune in deeply, to meet discomfort without resistance, and to trust that your body knows what it’s doing.

In a culture that prizes comfort and quick fixes, nausea disrupts the narrative. It’s a reminder that real change - whether in the mind, body, or spirit - often requires confronting shadows and sitting with mystery. Like the slow unfolding of new growth beneath winter’s frozen earth, nausea signals transformation beneath the surface.

This connects with what Michael Pollan explores in How to Change Your Mind, where the psychedelic experience is portrayed not as a magic wand but as a journey through complexity. The queasy moments aren’t side-effects but threads woven into the fabric of change.

When you can soften toward nausea, you’re practicing a form of mindfulness that carries beyond the session. You build trust in your body’s wisdom and resilience. You learn that discomfort is not a sign of failure but a threshold toward insight. This mindset shift transforms nausea from an obstacle into a doorway.

So next time nausea visits during a mushroom session, consider greeting it like an old, challenging friend. Sit with it, breathe with it, learn from it. In that space, you might discover the medicine isn’t just in the mushroom but in how you relate to your own body’s signals. And that, to me, is one of the quietest and most potent medicines of all.
